input.description: Get warning+ level logs from last 2 days related to sleep/reboot

input.command: journalctl --since "2 days ago" --until "now" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -E "Started.*Session|systemd\[1\]: Startup|kernel: Linux version|Reached target.*Sleep|systemd-sleep|Preparing for sleep|System Suspend|PM: suspend|PM: resume|ACPI: PM|Freezing|Thawing|reboot|shutdown|systemd-logind.*sleep|logind.*Sleep|logind.*Resume" | grep -v "UFW" | head -150
input.description: Get sleep/resume/boot events from last 2 days

input.command:
# Look at events around the crashes - specifically before the kernel reboots
# Crash 1: Mar 31 23:42 suspend → Apr 01 07:31 boot (no resume)
# Crash 2: Apr 01 16:13 suspend → freeze at 16:30 → boot at 16:31

journalctl --since "2026-04-01 16:25:00" --until "2026-04-01 16:35:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW BLOCK" | head -60
input.description: Look at events around the 16:30 crash

input.command:
# Look at the first crash context - what happened around Mar 31 23:42 suspend
journalctl --since "2026-03-31 23:38:00" --until "2026-04-01 07:35:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW BLOCK" | grep -v "^--$" | head -60

echo "---"
# Check last messages before crash
journalctl --since "2026-04-01 07:28:00" --until "2026-04-01 07:35:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW BLOCK" | head -30

input.command:
# Check sleep.conf and see what hibernate settings are configured
cat /etc/systemd/sleep.conf

echo "---"
# Also check if there's a hibernate timer configured
cat /etc/systemd/sleep.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || echo "(no drop-ins)"
input.description: Check systemd sleep configuration

content.content:
#  This file is part of systemd.
#
#  systemd is free software; you can redistribute it and/or modify it under the
#  terms of the GNU Lesser General Public License as published by the Free
#  Software Foundation; either version 2.1 of the License, or (at your option)
#  any later version.
#
# Entries in this file show the compile time defaults. Local configuration
# should be created by either modifying this file (or a copy of it placed in
# /etc/ if the original file is shipped in /usr/), or by creating "drop-ins" in
# the /etc/systemd/sleep.conf.d/ directory. The latter is generally
# recommended. Defaults can be restored by simply deleting the main
# configuration file and all drop-ins located in /etc/.
#
# Use 'systemd-analyze cat-config systemd/sleep.conf' to display the full config.
#
# See systemd-sleep.conf(5) for details.

[Sleep]
#AllowSuspend=yes
#AllowHibernation=yes
#AllowSuspendThenHibernate=yes
#AllowHybridSleep=yes
#SuspendState=mem standby freeze
HibernateState=disk
HibernateMode=shutdown
#MemorySleepMode=
#HibernateDelaySec=
HibernateOnACPower=no
#SuspendEstimationSec=60min
---
(eval):6: no matches found: /etc/systemd/sleep.conf.d/*.conf
(no drop-ins)

input.command:
# Check logind.conf for sleep/hibernate on lid close settings
cat /etc/systemd/logind.conf | grep -v "^#" | grep -v "^$"

echo "---"
# Check logind.conf.d dropins
ls /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d/ 2>/dev/null && cat /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || echo "(no drop-ins)"

echo "---"
# Check if suspend-then-hibernate is configured
systemd-analyze cat-config systemd/sleep.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^#" | grep -v "^$" | head -30
input.description: Check logind and sleep config for hibernate settings

content.content:
[Login]
HandlePowerKey=ignore
---
(no drop-ins)
---
[Sleep]
HibernateState=disk
HibernateMode=shutdown
HibernateOnACPower=no

input.command:
# Check what triggers suspend - Hyprland/lid close settings
cat /etc/systemd/logind.conf | grep -iE "lid|sleep|hibernate|suspend|handle"

echo "---"
# Check if there's a hypridle or swayidle config
find /home/xav/.config -name "hypridle*" -o -name "swayidle*" 2>/dev/null | head -5

echo "---"
# Check what the last boot reason was (last few boots)
journalctl --list-boots --no-pager 2>/dev/null | tail -10
input.description: Check lid close behavior and hypridle config

content.text: Now I have a complete picture. Let me also check the boot loop pattern from this morning:

input.command: journalctl --since "2026-04-02 09:00:00" --until "2026-04-02 09:10:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW BLOCK" | grep -E "kernel:|systemd\[1\]:|resume|hibernate|Startup|reboot|crash" | head -30

input.command:
# Check if there's a hibernate image / resume= kernel parameter that might explain boot loop
journalctl --since "2026-04-02 09:05:00" --until "2026-04-02 09:08:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -E "resume|hibernate|Failed|error|plymouth" | head -20

echo "---"
# Also check kernel cmdline for resume parameter
cat /proc/cmdline
input.description: Check hibernate resume and kernel cmdline

echo "---"

grep -i resume /etc/mkinitcpio.conf 2>/dev/null || echo "no resume hook in mkinitcpio.conf"
echo "---"
# Check systemd-hibernate-resume
systemctl status systemd-hibernate-resume 2>/dev/null | head -5

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Here's my analysis:

---

## Root Cause: `suspend-then-hibernate` enabled but hibernate not set up

**Machine:** Dell XPS 15 9500, BIOS 1.40.0, kernel 6.19.9-arch1-1, NVIDIA GPU

### What's happening

| Date | Sleep start | Duration | What happened |
|------|------------|----------|---------------|
| Mar 31 23:42 | → Apr 01 07:31 | 7.8h | Hard shutdown (hibernate attempt) |
| Apr 01 16:13 | → Apr 01 16:31 | 17min | Crashed during freeze phase |
| Apr 02 11:28 | → Apr 02 15:09 | 3.7h | Hard shutdown (hibernate attempt) |
| Apr 02 09:05–09:07 | — | — | 3-reboot boot loop |

Short sleeps (< ~10 min) all resume successfully. Long sleeps always end in a reboot.

### The mechanism

1. System enters **s2idle** (this machine has no S3, only Modern Standby)
2. **`suspend-then-hibernate`** is the default `SleepOperation` in systemd — after an estimated delay (based on battery drain), it triggers hibernate
3. Hibernate writes RAM to swap → then **`HibernateMode=shutdown`** (your config) issues a hard shutdown
4. On boot: **no `resume=` kernel param**, **no resume hook in mkinitcpio**, **only zram swap** (RAM-based — vanishes on power-off) → system just cold-boots instead of restoring

The Apr 01 16:30 crash is the clearest evidence: `Lid opened` and `Freezing user space processes` fire at the exact same timestamp — the hibernate freeze was in progress when you opened the lid, causing a race/crash. The `Freezing remaining freezable tasks completed` line is missing before the next kernel boot.

The Apr 01 08:13 boot that took **40min 53s in kernel stage** is the kernel/initramfs waiting (probably for a hibernate resume image or LUKS passphrase prompt) before timing out.

### The configuration problems in `/etc/systemd/sleep.conf`

```ini
HibernateState=disk     # DEPRECATED — ignored (hence the warning spam)
HibernateMode=shutdown  # Non-standard: shuts down instead of S4 ACPI
HibernateOnACPower=no   # Hibernate disabled on AC but enabled on battery
```

And swap is only **zram** — RAM-backed, disappears on shutdown, unusable for hibernate.

---

### Fix (simplest — disable the broken hibernate)

Edit `/etc/systemd/sleep.conf` and add:

```ini
AllowHibernation=no
AllowSuspendThenHibernate=no
```

Also remove or comment out the `HibernateState=disk` line (it's deprecated and generating warning spam). The file should look like:

```ini
[Sleep]
AllowHibernation=no
AllowSuspendThenHibernate=no
HibernateMode=shutdown
HibernateOnACPower=no
```

This stops systemd from ever attempting hibernate, so the system will stay in s2idle until you wake it manually or it drains the battery (at which point the firmware's own safety mechanism handles it).

### If you actually want hibernate to work

You'd need to:
1. Create a swap partition/file ≥ RAM size on persistent storage
2. Add `resume=<device>` to kernel cmdline in the bootloader
3. Add `resume` hook to `/etc/mkinitcpio.conf` and regenerate initramfs
4. Set a reasonable `HibernateDelaySec=` (e.g. `2h`)
5. Change `HibernateMode=` to `platform` (standard ACPI S4)

input.command:
# Focus on UCSI errors and TLP config - these are key
journalctl --since "2 days ago" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -iE "ucsi|UCSI|usbc|USB-C|PD[^F]|power.deliver" | grep -v "UFW" | grep -v "^--$" | sort -u | head -30

echo "==="
# Check TLP config for battery charge thresholds
grep -iE "charge|threshold|start|stop|bat|tlp" /etc/tlp.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^#" | grep -v "^$" | head -30

echo "==="
# Check if TLP is managing charging thresholds  
cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/charge_control_start_threshold 2>/dev/null || echo "no charge_control_start_threshold"
cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/charge_control_end_threshold 2>/dev/null || echo "no charge_control_end_threshold"
cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/status 2>/dev/null
cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/capacity 2>/dev/null
cat /sys/class/power_supply/AC/online 2>/dev/null
input.description: Check UCSI errors, TLP config, and current battery thresholds

input.command:
# Check TLP config for charge thresholds
grep -iE "START_CHARGE|STOP_CHARGE|BAT.*CHARGE|CHARGE.*BAT" /etc/tlp.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^#" | head -20

echo "==="
# Look for AC plug/unplug events with battery level context
journalctl --since "2 days ago" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -iE "AC:|online|Charging|Discharging|Full|power.supply" | grep -v "UFW" | grep -v "ACPI: Reserv" | grep -v "^--$" | head -50

echo "==="
# Check ucsi errors frequency - every boot?
journalctl --since "2 days ago" --no-pager 2>/dev/null | grep "ucsi_acpi" | grep -v "^--$"
input.description: Check TLP charge thresholds and AC plug events

input.command:
# Check UCSI errors around plug/unplug events more carefully
# The Apr 02 09:47, 15:44-15:48 clusters look interesting - correlate with AC state
journalctl --since "2026-04-02 09:40:00" --until "2026-04-02 10:00:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW BLOCK" | grep -iE "ucsi|AC:|online|charge|battery|plug|power|udev.*AC|omarchy" | head -30

echo "==="
journalctl --since "2026-04-02 15:40:00" --until "2026-04-02 15:55:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW BLOCK" | grep -iE "ucsi|AC:|online|charge|battery|plug|power|udev.*AC|omarchy" | head -30

echo "==="
# What udev rule runs omarchy-wifi-powersave when AC changes?
find /etc/udev/rules.d /usr/lib/udev/rules.d -name "*.rules" 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -l "omarchy\|powersave\|AC\|power_supply" 2>/dev/null | head -10
input.description: Look at AC plug/unplug events and udev rules

input.command:
cat /etc/udev/rules.d/99-wifi-powersave.rules
echo "==="
cat /etc/udev/rules.d/90-acpi-wakeup.rules
echo "==="
# TLP charge thresholds - check full TLP config
grep -iE "START_CHARGE|STOP_CHARGE|THRESHOLD|BAT.*CHARGE|CHARGE.*STOP|CHARGE.*START" /etc/tlp.conf 2>/dev/null | head -20
echo "==="
# Check if power-profiles-daemon is also running - TLP + ppd conflict?
systemctl is-active power-profiles-daemon 2>/dev/null
systemctl is-active tlp 2>/dev/null
input.description: Check udev rules and TLP/power-profiles-daemon conflict

===
# the charger, when booting the system or when executing 'tlp start':
# Battery Care -- Charge thresholds
# is below the start threshold. Charging stops when the charge level
# is above the stop threshold.
#   active and to look up vendor-specific threshold values. Some
# - If your hardware supports a start *and* a stop threshold, you must
#   specify both, otherwise TLP will refuse to apply the single threshold.
# - If your hardware supports only a stop threshold, set the start
# Battery charge level below which charging will begin.
#START_CHARGE_THRESH_BAT0=75
# Battery charge level above which charging will stop.
#STOP_CHARGE_THRESH_BAT0=80
# Battery charge level below which charging will begin.
#START_CHARGE_THRESH_BAT1=75
# Battery charge level above which charging will stop.
#STOP_CHARGE_THRESH_BAT1=80
# Restore charge thresholds when AC is unplugged: 0=disable, 1=enable.
#RESTORE_THRESHOLDS_ON_BAT=1
===

input.command:
# Where are the 50/90 thresholds coming from? Check dell-specific battery
cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/charge_control_start_threshold
cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/charge_control_end_threshold
echo "---"
# Check if any service/tool sets these thresholds
grep -r "50\|90\|threshold\|charge" /etc/tlp.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^#" | head -10
echo "---"
# Check tlp-stat for actual state
sudo tlp-stat -b 2>/dev/null | head -40
input.description: Check battery threshold source and TLP battery status

input.command: sudo tlp-stat -b 2>/dev/null | head -50
input.description: Run tlp-stat battery

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

input.command:
tlp-stat -b 2>&1 | head -50
echo "---"
# Check all sysfs battery info
ls /sys/class/power_supply/
echo "---"
for f in status capacity charge_control_start_threshold charge_control_end_threshold charge_type charge_now charge_full charge_full_design manufacturer model_name technology type; do
  val=$(cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/$f 2>/dev/null)
  [ -n "$val" ] && echo "$f: $val"
done
input.description: Get full battery status details

input.command:
# Check the ucsi power supply sources
for port in 1 2 3; do
  echo "=== USBC000:00$port ==="
  for f in type online current_max voltage_max input_current_limit; do
    val=$(cat /sys/class/power_supply/ucsi-source-psy-USBC000:00$port/$f 2>/dev/null)
    [ -n "$val" ] && echo "  $f: $val"
  done
done

echo "==="
# AC adapter status
for f in online type; do
  val=$(cat /sys/class/power_supply/AC/$f 2>/dev/null)
  [ -n "$val" ] && echo "AC $f: $val"
done

echo "==="
# Check what's in the UCSI log around plug events more carefully
journalctl --since "2026-04-02 22:28:00" --until "2026-04-02 22:35:00" --no-pager -o short-precise 2>/dev/null | grep -v "UFW" | grep -iE "ucsi|AC:|power_supply|charge|battery|usb.*hub|hub.*usb|tlp" | head -30

input.command:
# Interesting: UCSI port 2 shows online=1 at 20V/4.17A but AC=0!
# Is charger currently plugged in?
echo "AC online: $(cat /sys/class/power_supply/AC/online)"
echo "BAT status: $(cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/status)"
echo "BAT capacity: $(cat /sys/class/power_supply/BAT0/capacity)%"
echo ""
echo "UCSI ports:"
for port in 1 2 3; do
  online=$(cat /sys/class/power_supply/ucsi-source-psy-USBC000:00$port/online 2>/dev/null)
  current=$(cat /sys/class/power_supply/ucsi-source-psy-USBC000:00$port/current_max 2>/dev/null)
  voltage=$(cat /sys/class/power_supply/ucsi-source-psy-USBC000:00$port/voltage_max 2>/dev/null)
  echo "  Port $port: online=$online current_max=${current}µA voltage_max=${voltage}µV"
done

echo ""
echo "Last ACPI AC state in journal:"
journalctl --since "today" --no-pager 2>/dev/null | grep "AC Adapter\|AC:.*line\|online.*AC" | grep -v "UFW" | tail -5

content.text:
I can see this is happening **right now** — your charger is plugged in but the system is draining the battery. Here's the full diagnosis:

---

## The charging bug — confirmed live

```
UCSI Port 2: online=1  20V × 4.17A = 83W  ← charger physically delivering power
AC adapter:  online=0                       ← ACPI doesn't know about it
Battery:     Discharging at 19%             ← running on battery despite charger
```

**Root cause: `ucsi_acpi USBC000:00: UCSI_GET_PDOS failed (-5)`**

The UCSI driver fails to read Power Data Objects from the USB-C controller. When this fails, it doesn't update the ACPI AC adapter status (`/sys/class/power_supply/AC/online`), so the charging subsystem never activates — even though the charger is physically negotiated and delivering 83W.

This happens repeatedly across the 2 days, always in the same pattern: UCSI negotiates the port → `UCSI_GET_PDOS failed` → AC stays "offline" → battery drains.

### Quick fix for right now

Unplug and replug the charger, or try reloading the UCSI module:

```bash
sudo modprobe -r ucsi_acpi && sudo modprobe ucsi_acpi
```

### Secondary findings

**1. TLP + power-profiles-daemon conflict**

Both are installed and active simultaneously — this is explicitly unsupported. TLP fires from udev on every power event (tens of times per session) and warns it can't apply settings because `power-profiles-daemon` is also running. This creates redundant, conflicting power management.

TLP is not running as a service (`tlp.service` is disabled), but `power-profiles-daemon` is active. Pick one.

**2. Charge thresholds 50–90% (set in EC/BIOS)**

```
charge_control_start_threshold: 50
charge_control_end_threshold:   90
```

These are active at the kernel level even though TLP's config has them commented out — they were likely set previously and persisted in the Dell EC. At your current 19%, they wouldn't block charging (19% < 50%), but if your battery is between 50–90%, it won't charge by design. This is easy to mistake for a bug.

**3. Battery health: 58% of design capacity**

```
charge_full:        4,300,000 µAh  (actual)
charge_full_design: 7,394,000 µAh  (spec)
```

The battery holds only ~58% of its original capacity. With the 90% stop threshold, effective max charge is ~52% of the original spec (~3.87Ah). This makes the UCSI charging failures feel more severe since you start from a smaller reserve.

**4. `omarchy-wifi-powersave` script is missing**

Every AC plug/unplug event triggers a udev rule trying to run `/home/xav/.local/share/omarchy/bin/omarchy-wifi-powersave` which doesn't exist. This generates noise but doesn't affect charging directly.

---

### Summary of fixes

| Issue | Fix |
|-------|-----|
| UCSI charger not detected | Unplug/replug charger, or `modprobe -r ucsi_acpi && modprobe ucsi_acpi` |
| TLP/ppd conflict | Either `systemctl enable --now tlp` (and mask ppd) or remove TLP |
| Missing wifi powersave script | Restore omarchy or remove `/etc/udev/rules.d/99-wifi-powersave.rules` |

The UCSI issue is a kernel/firmware bug on this Dell XPS 9500 — it tends to occur after sleep/wake cycles (which you have many of), so fixing the hibernate crashes from before should also reduce frequency of the charging failures.

lastPrompt: there are some time where charging is not triggered even when charger is plugged
